question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a payment or order endpoint can be retried after a timeout and duplicate records sometimes appear. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Require an idempotency key, store the first completed result atomically, and return the stored outcome for repeated keys.
option_b: Make the endpoint use POST but log every retry so support can reverse accidental duplicates.
option_c: Check for duplicate-looking rows after insertion and merge them in a scheduled cleanup job.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a product page is slow but data changes only occasionally and stale content must be corrected quickly. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Move all product data into localStorage and refresh it only during login.
option_b: Disable caching everywhere so users always receive the newest server response.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a browser frontend on one domain calls an authenticated API on another domain and cookies are not being sent reliably. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where users remain logged in after token theft is suspected and access tokens are valid for a long time. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a full-stack app stores credentials and the team wants safer login without making the database unusable. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Base64-encode passwords before storing them so raw text is not visible in database tools.

option_c: Hash passwords quickly with a general-purpose hash to keep sign-in latency minimal.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a search endpoint builds a query from user filters and an attacker can change the WHERE clause. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?

option_d: Use environment-specific configuration and secret management, keeping secrets out of source control and images.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where a deployment adds a required database column while old application instances are still serving traffic. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Deploy the schema-breaking change first because migrations usually finish quickly.
option_b: Use backward-compatible expand-and-contract migrations and deploy code/data changes in safe phases.
option_c: Stop all traffic until every instance is updated and manually restart the database.
option_d: Rely on ORM auto-sync in production so the schema always matches the newest models.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where unit tests pass but releases still break communication between frontend, API, and external services. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Snapshot every rendered component and treat snapshots as full regression coverage.
option_b: Replace unit tests completely with manual QA because real flows matter most.
option_c: Add integration, contract, and end-to-end tests around critical boundaries while keeping unit tests for isolated logic.
option_d: Test only backend controllers because frontend bugs are visible immediately.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where real-time notifications work on one server but fail or duplicate when traffic is balanced across multiple instances. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Turn off load balancing whenever users need real-time updates.
option_b: Store all WebSocket connection objects in one server's memory and route traffic randomly.
option_c: Send the same notification repeatedly until every client acknowledges it visually.
option_d: Use shared pub/sub or message brokers, connection-aware routing, and idempotent event handling across instances.

question_text: A team is reviewing a full-stack feature where an Express API mixes thrown errors, rejected promises, and manual responses, causing inconsistent client failures. Which design choice is the strongest long-term fix?
option_a: Normalize errors through centralized middleware, async error propagation, typed error objects, and consistent response formats.
option_b: Wrap every route in a try/catch and return a different JSON shape for each failure.
option_c: Send raw stack traces to the browser so debugging is faster.
option_d: Let Node crash on unexpected errors and depend on the process manager to restart quickly.
